text: The surgeon places the epigastric port slightly to the left but angled towards the right. All four ports are introduced for the laparoscopic cholecystectomy. The fundus of the gallbladder is retracted towards the right shoulder, and minimal adhesions are separated using a harmonic scalpel.

The surgeon identifies the Rouviere's sulcus and performs posterior peritoneal dissection above it, carefully stripping down the peritoneum near the body of the gallbladder. Minimal omental adhesions are visible, and fatty tissue is dissected to skeletonize the cystic structures.

The surgeon carefully strips the fatty tissue from the body of the gallbladder, ensuring the dissection proceeds from above downwards to avoid excessive traction and prevent tinting of the gallbladder and common bile duct near the rovere sulcus.

The surgeon uses a Maryland dissector for blunt dissection, forming a posterior window between the cystic duct and cystic artery. The open jaw of the Maryland is visible, measuring approximately 2.2 centimeters, with the liver seen behind. The dissection continues to create a window between the cystic duct and artery, preparing for clipping rather than in-mass ligation.

The laparoscopic view shows the liver surface clearly visible in the superior aspect of Calot's triangle. The surgeon identifies the cystic duct, which appears slightly dilated, and clips the cystic artery. Only one clip is applied to the cystic artery, with plans to seal it distally using a harmonic device.

The surgeon applies three clips to the cystic duct, with precise spacing to ensure safe cutting between the second and third clips. The cystic artery is separated using a harmonic device to achieve hemostasis. Anterolateral and anteromedial traction is applied to maintain a proper dissection plane, with adjustments to the left hand for optimal traction.
The surgeon uses a hook instrument to separate the gallbladder from the liver, with the gallbladder almost completely detached. The final part is cut using a harmonic scalpel.
